nativeRange.habitat: Lagoons and seaward reefs from the surface down to roughly 50–60 m, with juveniles favoring isolated patches of branching coral or rubble in shallow sandy areas and adults occurring singly or in pairs on the slopes of deep lagoon or outer reefs.

decorPreferences: robust, well-secured live rock with caves and overhangs
no fragile decor — the fish will rearrange anything moveable
no exposed glass heaters, airline tubing, or unsecured equipment
sand substrate it can sift and blow

diet.notes: A robust benthic-invertebrate feeder. In the wild it crushes sea urchins, hard-shelled molluscs, crustaceans, tube worms, and pieces of branching coral, and is one of the few fish documented preying on crown-of-thorns starfish. In aquaria, a varied meaty diet is generally advised — krill, squid, clams, mussels, whole shrimp, chopped fish, and sea urchins. Hard-shelled items are commonly recommended several times per week to wear down the continuously growing teeth.

summary: The Titan Triggerfish is one of the largest triggerfish in the Indo-Pacific, capable of reaching 75 cm in length. It is widely considered one of the most aggressive species in the saltwater aquarium hobby and is generally regarded as not reef-safe. Keeping one successfully requires an exceptionally large fish-only system.
careNotes:
Most aquarium specimens settle in around 40–55 cm but should be planned for as if they will reach the full 75 cm. Lifespans are not tightly documented, but the species' size and resilience are consistent with 10–20 years in well-kept systems.

Tank requirements are extreme. A minimum of around 2,000 liters is generally recommended; smaller volumes may be managed short-term but tend to amplify aggression and leave no margin as the fish reaches its full growth potential.

Water chemistry is standard tropical reef: roughly 23–27°C, pH 8.0–8.4, specific gravity 1.021–1.025, and dKH 8–12. The Titan Triggerfish is generally considered tolerant of minor parameter swings once acclimated, though stable water quality is always preferable.

The Titan Triggerfish is widely regarded as not reef-safe. It actively crushes and consumes sea urchins, snails, crustaceans, tube worms, and corals, and will nip or destroy sessile invertebrates even when well fed. It is also widely reported to bite heaters, powerheads, airline tubing, and the keeper's hands — wild individuals are well documented attacking divers, particularly females guarding a nest, and the same intensity tends to carry over during maintenance work in captivity. Keepers are commonly advised to use long feeding tongs and to avoid placing bare hands in the tank.

The Titan Triggerfish is widely considered highly aggressive and should be kept one per system. Tankmates must be similarly large, robust, and assertive — large angelfish, moray eels, large wrasses, size-matched lionfish, and groupers are commonly cited as workable companions. Small, slow, peaceful, or shy species, other triggerfish, and any crustaceans or echinoderms should be avoided.

Diet should be varied, protein-rich, and include regular hard-shelled foods (whole shrimp, clams in shell, mussels, occasional sea urchins) to wear down the continuously growing teeth. Overgrown teeth can prevent feeding and become a husbandry emergency, so this aspect of care should not be neglected.

Several traits are worth noting. The dark moustache-like marking above the mouth gives the species one of its common names. A dorsal trigger spine can be locked erect, allowing the fish to wedge itself securely into rockwork for shelter. Wild-caught individuals from some parts of the Indo-Pacific have been associated with ciguatera (a toxin that accumulates in reef fish flesh and can cause illness in humans who eat them), though this is a food-safety concern for the fishing industry rather than a toxicity risk inside an aquarium. The species is assessed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List as of 2022. All aquarium-trade specimens are wild-caught.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is essentially unreported. In the wild, Titan Triggerfish are demersal spawners — meaning they lay eggs directly on the seafloor rather than releasing them into open water. A female excavates a shallow funnel-shaped nest in sand, deposits her eggs there, and aggressively guards the nest site, biting divers or any intruder that strays into the conical territory above it. Spawning occurs in roughly monthly cycles timed to the full or new moon, varying by population. Reproduction in aquaria is considered effectively impossible given the tank volumes and pair-bonding dynamics involved.

primary.notes: Species detail page (entered from the Triggerfish collection root https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/triggerfish supplied by the discovery manifest). Provided minimum tank (300 gal), care level (easy), temperament (aggressive), reef compatibility (no), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.0–8.4, SG 1.021–1.025, dKH 8–12), maximum size (30 in), diet, family (Balistidae), and behavioral notes (rearranges decor, hostile to divers, mustache marking).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name (Bloch & Schneider, 1801), taxonomy (Balistidae / Tetraodontiformes), max length 75.0 cm TL, wild temperature 25–29°C (mean 28.1°C), depth 0–60 m, Indo-Pacific distribution (Red Sea to Tuamotu), trophic level 3.3 ± 0.44, resilience suggesting population doubling time 4.5–14 years, hostile/territorial behavior toward divers when nest-guarding, and IUCN status Least Concern (Jan 2022).

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Tertiary corroboration only (not on the source whitelist for husbandry — used because designated secondaries BlueZoo Aquatics, Reef2Reef wiki, Fishkeeping World, and TFH Magazine all returned 403/404 or expired certs for this species). Confirmed max size 75 cm, depth to 50 m, Indo-Pacific range (absent from Hawaii), diet (urchins, molluscs, crustaceans, tube worms, coral, crown-of-thorns starfish), monthly lunar-tied spawning, dorsal-spine threat displays, and that bites are non-venomous but mechanically dangerous.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive-breeding records found. Demersal nest spawner in the wild, with monthly lunar-tied reproduction; aquarium reproduction is effectively unreported given tank-size and pair-dynamics demands.
